section.title: Job Curses the Day of His Birth
section.sectionRationale: After seven days of silence, Job finally speaks, and the prose narrator's measured voice gives way to raw, unfiltered poetry. This is the hinge between the calm prologue and the storm of the dialogue: Job does not curse God, as Satan predicted, but he curses the day of his birth, and every word here sets the agenda the three friends will spend the rest of the book answering, badly.
section.thesis: Job's grief finally breaks into speech, an anguished wish that he had never been born or had died at birth, exposing a man at the edge of endurance who still, even in his darkest words, does not curse God Himself.
section.frameNote: Three movements: Job's curse on the day and night of his conception and birth; his longing that he had died at birth and found the rest the dead enjoy; and his closing lament asking why light and life are given to a man in such misery, hedged in by God with no escape.

verses.text: After this, Job opened his mouth and {{qalal|cursed}} the day of his birth.
And this is what he said:

units.label: Let that day perish
units.note: may no light shine upon it

verses.text:
"May the day of my birth perish, and the night it was said, 
'A boy is conceived.'

If only that day had turned to darkness! May God above disregard it; 
may no light shine upon it.

May darkness and gloom reclaim it, and a cloud settle over it; 
may the blackness of the day overwhelm it.

If only darkness had taken that night away! May it not appear among the days of the year; 
may it never be entered in any of the months.
Behold, may that night be barren; may no joyful voice come into it.
May it be {{arar|cursed}} by those who curse the day, those prepared to rouse {{leviathan|Leviathan}}.

May its morning stars grow dark; may it wait in vain for daylight; 
may it not see the breaking of dawn.
For that night did not shut the doors of the womb to hide the sorrow from my eyes.

units.label: Why did I not die at birth
units.note: there the wicked cease from raging

verses.text: Why did I not perish at birth; why did I not die as I came from the womb?
Why were there knees to receive me, and breasts that I should be nursed?
For now I would be lying down in peace; I would be asleep and at rest
with kings and counselors of the earth, who built for themselves cities now in ruins,
or with princes who had gold, who filled their houses with silver.
Or why was I not hidden like a stillborn child, like an infant who never sees daylight?
There the wicked cease from raging, and there the weary find rest.
The captives enjoy their ease; they do not hear the voice of the oppressor.
Both small and great are there, and the slave is freed from his master.

units.label: Why is light given to the miserable

verses.text: Why is light given to the miserable, and life to the bitter of soul,
who long for death that does not come, and search for it like hidden treasure,
who rejoice and greatly exult when they can find the grave?
Why is life given to a man whose way is hidden, whom God has {{sakak|hedged}} in?
I sigh when food is put before me, and my groans pour out like water.
For the thing I feared has overtaken me, and what I dreaded has befallen me.
I am not at ease or quiet; I have no rest, for trouble has come."

qalal.gloss: To curse, treat as light or worthless. Job does not curse God, as Satan predicted he would; instead he curses the day of his own birth, wishing it counted for nothing among the days of the year. Grief here turns inward, against his own existence, not upward against his Maker.

arar.gloss: To curse with a binding oath, a stronger and more formal term than qalal. Job invokes professional cursers, those who rouse Leviathan, to bind his birth-day under a solemn curse, escalating the intensity of his opening lament.

leviathan.gloss: A great sea monster representing untamed chaos, later described at length by God Himself in chapters 40 to 41. Job's invocation of those who can rouse Leviathan pictures the darkest, most chaotic forces imaginable, called upon to swallow up the day of his birth.

sakak.gloss: To hedge in, fence about, enclose. Satan complained in 1:10 that God had hedged Job in for protection and blessing; here Job uses the same picture bitterly, feeling hedged in not for safety but for suffering, with no way out. The same fence looks like grace from one side and a trap from the other.

basic.context: Job finally breaks his silence, and what pours out is not the composed worship of chapter 1 but raw anguish. He curses the day of his birth, wishing it had never been counted among the days of the year, and wonders why he did not simply die at birth, where he imagines the rest that even kings and slaves share equally in death. He closes by asking why life and light are given at all to someone in such bitter misery, a man who feels fenced in by God with no way out.
basic.hermeneutics: This chapter gives permission for believers to lament without pretending. Job does not curse God, exactly what Satan predicted he would do; he curses his own existence instead, which is a different and, the book implies, permissible kind of speech. The friends will soon treat this outburst as evidence of guilt needing correction; the reader, who has seen the heavenly council, knows better.
basic.originalLanguages: Job uses qalal, curse or treat as worthless, for his own birth-day, then escalates to arar, a formal binding curse, invoking those who can rouse Leviathan, the great chaos monster. And he complains that God has him sakak, hedged in, the very word Satan used in 1:10 to describe God's protective fence around Job; the same word now feels like a trap rather than a shelter.
basic.typology: Job's wish that he had died at birth and found rest among the dead echoes forward into the Psalms' laments and anticipates the deep cry of dereliction that will be voiced perfectly, and without any hint of sin, by Christ Himself in His darkest hour.
basic.christ: Job's language here, though never crossing into cursing God, comes closer to despair than anywhere else in the book, and Scripture does not rebuke him for it. That same raw honesty before God finds its deepest echo in Gethsemane and at Golgotha, where Christ cried out in real anguish, yet without sin, showing that lament this dark can still be faithful when it is brought to God rather than turned against Him.
basic.god: God is strikingly absent from this chapter as a direct address; Job speaks about God, hedged in by Him, but does not yet speak to Him as he will in later chapters. The silence is part of the honesty: sometimes suffering first comes out sideways, as complaint about existence itself, before it becomes prayer.
basic.crossReferences: Jeremiah 20:14-18 echoes this chapter almost verbatim, cursing the day of his own birth; Psalm 88 gives a whole psalm of unrelieved lament with no resolving verse; Matthew 26:38 records Christ's soul as deeply grieved, even to the point of death.
basic.oneStory: Job's opening speech sets the tone for the whole middle of the book: honest, unresolved anguish that never quite becomes rebellion against God. It gives later sufferers, including the church, permission to bring their darkest words to God rather than suppressing them, and it points toward a Savior who entered that same darkness fully.

context.literary: Chapter 3 is the literary hinge of the book, moving from measured prose narrative to raw poetic lament, and its structure of three movements, curse, wish for death, and complaint against life, will recur in various forms throughout the speeches that follow.
context.historical: Ancient Near Eastern lament literature includes similar 'why was I born' complaints, but Job's version is unusually extended and theologically loaded, framed by a book that has already told the reader the real cause of his suffering, unlike similar laments in surrounding cultures.
context.canonical: Jeremiah will later echo this chapter's language almost exactly in his own confession, showing that Scripture treats this kind of raw lament as a legitimate, repeatable form of speech to God, not an isolated aberration confined to Job.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that grief this deep can be spoken honestly without becoming sin, giving voice to the darkest thoughts of a suffering believer before any friend offers a single word of counsel.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Job's lament; it does not prescribe wishing one had never been born as a normal or required response to suffering. What it does establish as abiding truth is that such raw honesty, brought before God rather than turned into cursing Him, is not itself sinful.

debates.question: Does Job sin in chapter 3 by cursing the day of his birth?

views.label: No open sin, but the edge of what is permissible
views.case: The narrator has twice already said Job did not sin; nothing in this chapter reverses that verdict, and Job curses his day, not his God, throughout.

views.label: A dangerous drift that the rest of the book will need to correct
views.case: God's later speeches from the whirlwind will rebuke Job for words that darken counsel without knowledge, suggesting that even here Job's grief begins to overreach into territory that needs correcting by the book's end.

debates.bearing: Whichever view is taken, the chapter itself stops short of narrating any explicit sin, and its main function is to give raw lament a legitimate place in Scripture rather than to settle every question about Job's later overreach.

study.originalLanguages: Job's opening curse uses qalal, to treat as worthless, escalating to arar, a formal binding curse invoking those who rouse Leviathan, the chaos monster later described by God Himself. His closing complaint that God has sakak, hedged, him in reuses the very word Satan spoke of God's protective fence in 1:10, now felt as a trap rather than a shelter, capturing in one word how differently the same providence can feel from the inside of suffering.

typology.type: Job's wish for the rest of death, where the wicked cease from raging and the weary find rest
typology.fulfillment: The true rest offered in Christ, who gives rest to the weary not by ending life but by bearing its burden, Matthew 11:28.

typology.type: Job's unanswered lament, spoken honestly to no apparent reply
typology.fulfillment: Christ's cry of dereliction on the cross, My God, my God, why have you forsaken me, the deepest and only truly innocent lament in Scripture.

study.christ: Job's lament here is real anguish that stops short of sin, an honest cry into apparent silence. That same kind of cry, far darker and entirely undeserved, is voiced by Christ Himself, who was truly forsaken so that no lament of His people would ever go finally unanswered. Job's longing for rest, where the weary find rest and the wicked cease from raging, is answered not by the grave he wished for but by the greater rest Christ offers to all who are weary and burdened.
study.god: God does not speak in this chapter, and Job does not yet speak directly to Him either; the silence itself is part of what the book is teaching, that faith can hold even when no answer comes and no explanation is given. God's absence here is not the same as His absence in fact, as the reader, who has seen the council in heaven, already knows.

crossReferences.note: Jeremiah curses the day of his own birth in language strikingly close to Job 3, showing this kind of lament recurs legitimately in Scripture.
A psalm of unrelieved lament with no resolving verse of praise, showing that Scripture makes room for grief that does not resolve within the poem itself.
Christ's soul is deeply grieved, even to the point of death, in Gethsemane, the deepest and sinless echo of this kind of honest anguish.

study.oneStory: Job's first words after silence are a curse on his own birth-day, not on God, and they set the honest, unresolved tone for the whole middle of the book. This raw permission to lament without pretending points forward to a Savior who entered the deepest darkness of forsakenness Himself, so that no honest cry of His people is ever finally unanswered.

sermon.title: Holding on to Your Faith in the Midst of Suffering: Job, Part 2
sermon.why: Piper works through Job's explosion in chapter 3 at length, quoting 3:1-3, 3:11-12, and 3:20-21 directly, framing it as the moment Job's suffering drags on past the initial crisis grace and his faith begins the long struggle the rest of the book will trace.

quiz.q: What does Job actually curse in chapter 3?

options.text: The day of his own birth, not God Himself.
options.note: Job curses his birth-day throughout the chapter; he never curses God, contrary to Satan's prediction in chapters 1 and 2.

options.text: The night of his conception, wishing it had never been recorded among the days of the year.
options.note: A true and specific detail from the chapter's opening curse, though less complete than naming the whole target as his birth-day throughout.

options.text: His three friends for their silence.
options.note: Job says nothing critical of his friends' silence in this chapter; his words are aimed at his own existence.

options.text: His wife's earlier counsel.
options.note: Chapter 3 does not address his wife at all; that exchange occurred in chapter 2.

quiz.q: What does Job long for in verses 11 to 19?

options.text: That he had died at birth and found the rest that even kings, slaves, and the wicked share equally in death.
options.note: This is the substance of his wish throughout this section, picturing death as a great equalizer of rest.

options.text: That he had never been born at all, since even a stillborn child escapes the sorrow he now feels.
options.note: A true part of Job's lament in this section, though less complete than the fuller picture of death's rest shared by all people alike.

options.text: That his children could be brought back to life.
options.note: Job's lament here is about his own birth and death, not a specific request to restore his children.

options.text: That his wealth would be instantly restored.
options.note: This section makes no mention of restored wealth; it concerns the rest of the grave.

quiz.q: What is the significance of Job's complaint that God has 'hedged him in' (3:23)?

options.text: It bitterly echoes Satan's own words in 1:10, where the same hedge described God's protection; the same fence now feels like a trap rather than a shelter.
options.note: The reuse of the same Hebrew image, sakak, links the two chapters and shows how differently the same providence can feel from inside suffering.

options.text: It shows Job feels completely enclosed by God's providence with no way to escape his suffering.
options.note: True to Job's feeling in the moment, though less precise than identifying the deliberate echo of Satan's own words in 1:10.

options.text: It has no connection to any earlier part of the book.
options.note: The connection to Satan's words in 1:10 is a deliberate and significant echo, even if easy to miss on a first reading.

options.text: It refers to a literal fence around Job's property.
options.note: The image is figurative, describing God's providential enclosure of Job's life, not a literal boundary.

quiz.q: Why is it significant that Job addresses no words directly to God in this chapter?

options.text: It shows suffering can first come out sideways, as complaint about existence itself, before it becomes direct prayer, which Job will offer in later chapters.
options.note: This chapter's silence toward God, contrasted with his direct address in later speeches, marks a real stage in how his grief develops.

options.text: It shows Job is still assuming God's active hand governs his life, even while not yet speaking to Him directly.
options.note: True and important, though less complete than naming the specific developmental shift toward direct address in the chapters that follow.

options.text: It means the chapter has no theological content.
options.note: The chapter is deeply theological even without direct address to God, especially in its assumption that God governs Job's suffering.

options.text: It proves Job has abandoned belief in God entirely.
options.note: Nothing in the chapter suggests unbelief; Job assumes God's active hand throughout, even while not addressing Him directly.

quiz.q: How does this chapter's honesty about suffering function within the whole book?

options.text: It gives legitimate scriptural voice to the darkest thoughts of a suffering believer, brought to God rather than turned into cursing Him.
options.note: This is the chapter's enduring theological contribution, matched by similar honest lament elsewhere in Scripture such as Jeremiah 20 and Psalm 88.

options.text: It shows that raw, unfiltered grief has a legitimate place within a life of genuine faith.
options.note: Correct in substance, though less precise than noting that this lament is spoken about existence rather than turned into cursing God directly.

options.text: It is simply narrative filler with no theological purpose.
options.note: The chapter does real theological work, modeling honest lament as a legitimate form of faith under extreme pressure.

options.text: It teaches that strong emotion in suffering is always sinful and to be suppressed.
options.note: The chapter's placement in inspired Scripture, without narrated rebuke, argues against this reading.

questions: Job's lament pours out sideways, about his own existence, before it ever becomes direct prayer to God. Where in your own grief have you found it easier to complain about life in general than to speak honestly to God Himself?
Job curses his birth-day but never God. What is the difference, in your own experience, between honest lament and actually turning against God in your heart?

The same word, hedge, described God's protection in chapter 1 and now describes what feels like a trap in chapter 3. Has God's providence ever felt like both to you at different times, and how did you hold on through that shift?

This chapter offers no resolution, no answer, no comfort by its final verse. How comfortable are you leaving a prayer or a lament unresolved before God, rather than rushing to tidy it up with an answer you do not actually have?

Job's friends will soon treat this outburst as evidence of guilt. Where have you, or someone who cared for you, mistaken raw grief for sin that needed correcting rather than simply needing to be heard?

translationNotes.note: Cursed renders qalal, treat as light or worthless, distinct from the stronger arar used later in verse 8; the shift in verbs marks an escalation within the chapter's opening curse.

Those prepared to rouse Leviathan likely refers to professional sorcerers or cursers believed capable of summoning cosmic chaos monsters to devour the sun or day, a vivid ancient image for the deepest possible curse.
Hedged in translates the same Hebrew root, sakak or suk, used by Satan in 1:10 to describe God's protective fence around Job; the repetition is a deliberate and bitter irony in the Hebrew text.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Job 3:1-26. The em dash in verse 8 of the source text was replaced with a comma per house style; no other substantive changes were made to the verse text.
